* ValueError

It's not Python's convention to use new exceptions where existing
exceptions will suffice. We should just use the new exception where we
can't sufficiently use one of the well-known ones. For example, we
shouldn't use ValueError from the lower levels because it could take on
multiple interpretations. However, someone passing a value that does not
exist within the discrete set of potential choices would understand exactly
what a ValueError was trying to tell them.

Plus, given its name, an SvnException can be used just to describe SVN
command failures.

Dustin

Sure. But note this will invoke the git subprocess even when either environment variable is set.

I haven't tried to support it yet (for now I'm using git shallow clones), but AppVeyor can actually get the source from github without doing a git clone.  And I believe that in that case one needs to get all the info from the [environment variables](https://www.appveyor.com/docs/environment-variables), particularly all `APPVEYOR_REPO_COMMIT_*` ones.  Any attempt to get the info through git command will spew error messages like `fatal: Not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git`.

I don't like the nested try... except much either myself, but it was the best way I could come up that avoids the git subprocess.

Anyway, I don't care much either way now, since I'm using a git shallow clone, since it's necessary for all other fields, and I'm not sure it's worth trying to support AppVeyor clone-less mode.

static thread_local is to create an object once for each thread. Keeping this statement in a function is to help reference the object from different functions (i.e., forward and backward functions).

Init() has to be called every time the forward/backward function is invoked. Because the current operator interface is stateless. This is the best way to initialize the state for the followup operations in the forward/backward.

@joguSD don't think there is another event the stubber could rely on. This PR is having the presigners emit`provide-client-params` and `before-parameter-build` which are the events that would contain the unserialized parameters. Any of the other events down the stack use the serialized version of the parameters. So if we were to hook into another event, we would need to create a new one which we would not want to do.

@jamesls One idea initially that I have is to inject a value into the `context` object a flag like `is_presign_request` in the presigner logic and then in the Stubber class we can check for this flag and pass over asserting the request if the presign flag is present in the context. What's your thoughts on this? Not sure if there really any better options given the stubber currently does not have a way to disambiguate between a presign request and a normal client request.

[numpy.random_integers](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.random.random_integers.html) selects from a closed range, [0,3] here, while [numpy.randint](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.random.randint.html) selects from a half-open range, [0,3) here.

Since the former is now deprecated (why, numpy?), I think you want to change the argument to `(0,4,n_samples)` for `randint`.

to_vc_interface should be used privately within the VC to create a new memory object which is identical to the one that was passed into the VC by the user. This has the effect of enabling read/write permission for all read/write operations to the memory when using the new value returned by to_vc_interface. This is to make it easier to support the two use cases of reading/writing memory from the test bench without permission checks and having axi_slave etc. perform memory operations with permission checks.

The fact we have to catch and raise an exception here indicates to me that we're not going about this the best way. For example,  [this code](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/v0.6.0/acme/acme/client.py#L163) uses `update_registration` without raising an exception.

I played with it a bit and was able to update a registration using this code:
```
acme_client.acme.update_registration(
    acc.regr.update(body=acc.regr.body.update(contact=('mailto:' + config.email,))))
```
While absolutely horrendous, it works and seems to be the way that `update_registration` was designed to work. Additionally, this function returns the updated registration so we don't need the additional call and trip to Boulder with `query_registration`.
